title: Kinds of Data We Collect and Share
obj: Students will be able to inventory the kinds of data internet users collect and share, and link each to a real-world AI use.
content: messages, posts, search queries, reviews, documents — powers NLP, search, sentiment
photos, social uploads, livestreams, surveillance — powers CV, facial recognition, content moderation
calls, voice assistants, songs — powers speech-to-text, voice cloning, music recommendation
clicks, scrolls, dwell time, purchases — powers recommendation and advertising engines
GPS traces, check-ins, map queries — powers navigation, rideshare, geomarketing
wearables, smart-home devices, industrial sensors — powers fitness AI and predictive maintenance
assess: Inventory exercise: list 10 pieces of data your phone has collected about you in the last hour
Map: for each of the 6 data types, name one AI product that uses it
Reflection: which kind of data about yourself are you most protective of, and why?

title: Basic Data Types Computers Use
obj: Students will be able to name the fundamental data types computers work with and identify the appropriate type for a given value.
content: integers for counts, floats for measurements; why you don't use floats for money
characters and strings; Unicode support; common encodings (UTF-8)
stored as numbers but displayed as formatted strings; timezone complexity
bitmap (raster) vs vector; pixel values; file formats (PNG, JPEG, SVG)
sampled waveforms; sample rate and bit depth; formats (WAV, MP3, FLAC)
True/False; used for flags, toggles, decision branches
assess: Classify 10 example values (e.g., 3.14, 'Ada', 2026-04-17, a smiley image) by their data type
Short answer: why is representing money as a float dangerous?
Lab: examine a spreadsheet column and identify its data type

title: Data vs. Information
obj: Students will be able to distinguish raw data from actionable information and explain what context adds.
content: raw, unorganized facts and measurements — a list of numbers with no labels is data
data placed in a context that makes it useful — same numbers labeled 'weekly sales, Store 5' is information
adding labels, units, relationships, or comparisons converts data into information
data → information → knowledge → wisdom (DIKW pyramid); each layer adds interpretation
models train on raw data but produce outputs that are information (predictions in context)
assess: Given 3 pieces of raw data, turn each into information by adding context
Short answer: how do labels in a dataset convert data to information?
Analyze: review a news chart and identify what context makes the numbers meaningful

title: The Data Processing Cycle
obj: Students will be able to diagram the input–processing–output cycle and apply it to a real data-processing task.
content: collect data: forms, sensors, APIs, files, user actions — validate before accepting
transform data: clean, filter, aggregate, compute; this is where value is added
deliver results: reports, dashboards, files, API responses, user-facing predictions
between stages data is saved; databases, files, caches; affects cost and latency
outputs often inform the next cycle (e.g., predictions drive decisions that generate new data)
assess: Diagram: draw the input-process-output-feedback cycle for a weather-forecast service
Apply: walk through the data processing cycle for a food-delivery app's ETA prediction
Short answer: why does every processing step risk introducing errors?

title: Bits, Bytes, and How Computers Store Data
obj: Students will be able to explain how computers represent data in binary and convert small values between decimal and binary.
content: binary digit: 0 or 1; the smallest unit; electrically an off/on state
8 bits = 256 possible values; enough for a single ASCII character or a small number
find highest power of 2 that fits, subtract, repeat
KB, MB, GB, TB — each ~1,000 of the prior (or 1,024 in strict 'binary' units)
everything — images, audio, text — is ultimately bits; model sizes in GB mean billions of bytes
assess: Convert: 13 in decimal = ? in binary; 1010 in binary = ? in decimal
Lab: use Python bin() and int() to check your conversions
Short answer: why is a 'byte' 8 bits and not, say, 10?

title: Big Data and Why AI Needs It
obj: Students will be able to define Big Data using the '5 Vs' and explain why modern AI is data-hungry.
content: terabytes to petabytes; web-scale datasets like Common Crawl, YouTube corpora
streaming data from sensors, clicks, financial feeds — must process in near real-time
mixed formats (text + image + sensor) that require different tools to unify
quality and trustworthiness; noisy data is everywhere; cleaning is 80% of DS work
the payoff — what business/societal decisions the data unlocks
modern ML models have billions of parameters; they need billions of examples to generalize
assess: Apply: pick a product (e.g., Netflix) and describe its data through each of the 5 Vs
Short answer: why can't a deep-learning model trained on 100 images compete with one trained on 10 million?
Research: find one real dataset with stated size (rows, GB) and classify each V

title: How AI Uses Data to Make Predictions
obj: Students will be able to explain at a high level how data flows through an ML model to produce predictions or decisions.
content: show the algorithm many labeled examples so it learns statistical patterns
a function that takes new input and returns a prediction; the 'parameters' encode what it learned
running a trained model on new data; much cheaper than training
a model outputs probabilities or best guesses; always evaluate accuracy against ground truth
real-world data collected after deployment can retrain the model — careful with bias amplification
assess: Diagram: sketch how training data flows into a model and how new predictions come out
Short answer: why is a model's prediction never '100% certain' even on training data?
Scenario: a spam filter starts flagging legitimate mail — describe what might have caused this drift

title: Boolean Logic in Programming and AI
obj: Students will be able to evaluate boolean expressions, build truth tables, and recognize how boolean logic appears in AI decision rules.
content: the two boolean values; every comparison reduces to one or the other
truth tables; precedence (NOT > AND > OR); parens to be safe
exactly-one-is-true; used in cryptography and some neural-network training
NOT (A AND B) == (NOT A) OR (NOT B); a useful refactoring tool
expert systems chain boolean conditions; a leaf in a decision tree is a boolean test
assess: Build the truth table for `(A AND B) OR (NOT C)`
Simplify: use De Morgan's law to rewrite `NOT (x > 0 AND x < 10)`
Short answer: how does a decision tree use boolean logic?

title: Spreadsheet Fundamentals for Data Work
obj: Students will be able to organize different kinds of data in a spreadsheet, apply built-in functions, and format for readability.
content: one row = one record, one column = one attribute; headers in row 1
numbers, text, dates, formulas — watch out for auto-converted dates and leading-zero loss
SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, MIN, MAX, IF, VLOOKUP/XLOOKUP, CONCAT
sort by one or more columns; filter to focus on a subset
number format (currency, %, date), conditional formatting to highlight outliers
arrow keys + Ctrl/Cmd + Shift for fast navigation and selection
assess: Lab: import a 50-row dataset, sort by a numeric column, and apply conditional formatting to outliers
Write 3 formulas: sum a column, count non-blank cells, look up a value by id
Troubleshoot: given a CSV where dates imported as numbers, fix the formatting

title: Advanced Spreadsheet Features: Pivots, Formulas, Cell References
obj: Students will be able to use pivot tables, structured references, and advanced formulas to analyze data in a spreadsheet.
content: drag-and-drop summarization: rows, columns, values, filters; instant group-bys
count, sum, average, % of total, running total — switch via 'Summarize Values By'
$A$1 locks both, $A1 locks column, A$1 locks row — critical for dragging formulas
SUMIF, COUNTIF, AVERAGEIF — aggregate with a condition
XLOOKUP (modern), VLOOKUP (legacy); how to match across two sheets
rule of thumb: >100k rows or multi-step transforms → move to Python/Pandas
assess: Lab: build a pivot table from a sales dataset showing sum-of-revenue by region and year
Write a SUMIF formula that totals sales only for one product category
Short answer: why would you use XLOOKUP instead of VLOOKUP?

title: Data Visualization Basics
obj: Students will be able to pick the right chart type for a dataset and build readable visuals in a spreadsheet or Python.
content: bar = compare categories, line = change over time, scatter = correlation, histogram = distribution, pie = shares (sparingly)
clear axis labels, honest scales, legible font, single headline question per chart
truncated y-axis, 3D effects, dual axes, overly-small pie slices — common ways to mislead
Excel/Sheets charts, Python matplotlib/seaborn, Tableau, Power BI, D3 for custom web
less flashy but powerful for small datasets; show distribution without binning
assess: Pick the right chart: given 4 datasets, name the best chart type and justify
Lab: create a bar chart and a line chart in a spreadsheet; fix 2 labeling problems
Critique: find a misleading chart online and identify the technique used

title: Analyzing Data to Construct Decisions
obj: Students will be able to analyze a dataset and construct informed summaries, decisions, or predictions with appropriate caveats.
content: what decision will this analysis inform? be specific about the outcome
mean, median, mode, range, standard deviation — summarize the dataset
group-bys and comparisons reveal differences between segments
smoothing, moving averages, year-over-year — what direction is the metric going?
sample size, missing data, outliers, confounders — always flag
state the finding, then the recommended action, then the uncertainty
assess: Lab: given a school-enrollment dataset, compute 3 summary stats and one actionable finding
Critique: review a report and identify missing caveats
Design: write a 1-page analysis for a school-leader decision using the structure above
